items.mistake: Applying GNB to raw categorical or count text features without encoding
items.fix: Use appropriate models: one-hot + consider Gaussian only if treated as numeric with care, or switch to Multinomial/Bernoulli Naive Bayes for discrete counts.
items.why: GNB assumes continuous Normal likelihoods per feature — arbitrary category codes are not Gaussian measurements.
items.example: Wrong: GaussianNB on raw city IDs {1,2,3}. Right: one-hot encode or use MultinomialNB on word counts.

items.mistake: Ignoring feature correlation within a class
items.fix: Apply PCA or feature selection, try discriminative models, or accept bias from the naive product — see Conditional Independence Assumption for when it hurts.
items.why: GNB multiplies per-feature scores as if features were unrelated given y — correlated inputs double-count evidence.
items.example: Height and weight jointly indicate class; separate Gaussians treat a tall-heavy profile as two independent surprises.

items.mistake: Skipping variance smoothing or handling zero-variance features
items.fix: Use library defaults (sklearn var_smoothing) or add epsilon to σ²; inspect clf.var_ after fit.
items.why: If all class-k points share the same value on feature j, σ²=0 makes the Gaussian PDF a spike — infinite log density at one point, zero elsewhere.
items.example: All class-0 rows have feature j=3.0 → var_=0 → predict_proba becomes NaN without smoothing.

items.mistake: Multiplying PDF values in raw probability space
items.fix: Sum log priors and log Gaussian PDFs, then argmax — use predict_log_proba in sklearn.
items.why: Many features multiply many small PDF numbers → numerical underflow to 0.
items.example: Wrong: product of 20 PDFs each ~0.01. Right: sum of 20 log PDFs ≈ 20 * log(0.01).
